answers.Scope?: Hermes first; OpenClaw as a fast-follow using the same adapter pattern.

non_goals: Session-id capture / --resume delta turns for Hermes (the Path B optimization — follow-up once a live install confirms the id-capture mechanism)
Changing the connector's claude/codex/gemini adapters or the routing/coverage model (unified-connections already shipped)
acceptance_criteria: A connector with the 'hermes' CLI installed reports hermes in detected_providers (shutil.which('hermes'))

When the server routes a turn to a hermes-provider agent, the connector invokes 'hermes -z' one-shot with the full game state and parses the move from stdout; no --model is passed (Hermes uses its own configured model)
Every hermes turn sends full state (Path A) — no session id is captured or required; failover and first-turn behave identically
A hermes agent is created with no model dropdown (provider=hermes); routing/coverage treat hermes like any other provider
Unit tests mock the hermes subprocess (success + malformed output -> connector fallback move); a live-install validation step is documented

checklist.goal: Add a connector adapter so Hermes (NousResearch/hermes-agent) agents play via the standard low-token connector — invoked one-shot per turn (hermes -z), full game state each turn (Path A: no session resume yet). OpenClaw is a fast-follow.
checklist.audience: Players who run Hermes as their AI competitor; they install the connector once per machine and toggle the Hermes provider on, like claude/gemini/openai today.
checklist.constraints: Hermes 'hermes -z' headless mode: single prompt in, final reply text out, exit. Watch NousResearch/hermes-agent#38252 (clean CLI handback). No live Hermes install is available to the implementer yet, so the adapter is written against the documented CLI + mocked-subprocess tests; a live -z smoke test is a required pre-merge step (data/integration-critical).
